request.question: Mia Hansen-Løve won the Silver Bear for Best Director for a film staring whom?

title: Norbert Kückelmann
45 Years
All Is Forgiven (film)
Things to Come (2016 film)
Father of My Children
Goodbye First Love
Mia Hansen-Løve
Kim Ki-duk
Alice de Lencquesaing
Eden (2014 French film)
sentences: Norbert Kückelmann, (born 1 May 1930 - died 31 August 2017) was a German film director, screenwriter and lawyer.
He was born in Munich, During the 1950s he studied law and worked part-time as a film critic.
After graduation, he worked as a lawyer in Munich and Mainz.
In 1965 he founded together with Alexander Kluge and Hans-Rolf Strobel the Young German Film Committee (German: "Kuratorium Junger Deutscher Film" .
Continuing to work as a lawyer he directed his first film "Die Sachverständigen" in 1973.
At the 23rd Berlin International Film Festival the film won a Silver Bear.
His first film also won the Deutscher Filmpreis - Best Feature Film.
At the 34th Berlin International Film Festival, his film "Man Under Suspicion" also won a Silver Bear.
Two years later, he was a member of the jury at the 36th Berlin International Film Festival.
45 Years is a 2015 British romantic drama film directed and written by Andrew Haigh.
The film is based on the short story "In Another Country" by David Constantine.
The film was screened in the main competition section of the 65th Berlin International Film Festival.
Charlotte Rampling won the Silver Bear for Best Actress and Tom Courtenay won the Silver Bear for Best Actor.
At the 88th Academy Awards, Rampling received a nomination for Best Actress in a Leading Role.
All Is Forgiven (French: Tout est pardonné ) is a 2007 French drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It was screened in the Directors’ Fortnight section of the 2007 Cannes Film Festival.
It won the Louis Delluc Prize for Best First Film.
It was also nominated for the César Award for Best First Feature Film at the 2008 César Awards.
Things to Come (French title: L'Avenir) is a 2016 French-German drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It stars Isabelle Huppert as middle-aged philosophy professor Nathalie Chazeaux, whose life is going through a series of separations.
Father of My Children (French: Le Père de mes enfants ) is a 2009 French drama film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It won the Jury Special Prize in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2009 Cannes Film Festival.
It is based in part on the life of the late Humbert Balsan.
Goodbye First Love (French: Un amour de jeunesse ) is a 2011 Franco-German film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.
It was selected for the main competition section at the 2011 Locarno International Film Festival.
Mia Hansen-Løve (born 5 February 1981) is a French film director, screenwriter and former actress.
Her film "Father of My Children", won the Special Jury Prize in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2009 Cannes Film Festival.
In 2016, she won the Silver Bear for Best Director for her film "Things to Come" at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival.
Kim Ki-duk ( ] ; born December 20, 1960) is a South Korean filmmaker noted for his idiosyncratic art-house cinematic works.
His films have received many distinctions in the festival circuit, making him one of the most important contemporary Asian film directors.

Major festival awards include Golden Lion at 69th Venice International Film Festival for "Pietà", Silver Lion for Best Director at 61st Venice International Film Festival for "3-Iron", Silver bear for Best Director at 54th Berlin International Film Festival for "Samaria" and Un Certain Regard prize at 2011 Cannes Film Festival for Arirang.
His most widely known feature is "Spring, Summer, Fall, Winter... and Spring" (2003), included in film critic Roger Ebert's Great Movies.
Two of his films served as official submissions for Academy award for best foreign language film as South Korean entries.
He has given scripts to several of his former assistant directors including Juhn Jai-hong ("Beautiful" and "Poongsan") and Jang Hoon ("Rough Cut").
Alice de Lencquesaing (born 11 August 1991) is a French actress who appeared in Mia Hansen-Løve's 2009 film "Father of My Children" with her father Louis-Do de Lencquesaing.
She was also in the film "Summer Hours".
Eden is a 2014 French drama film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve and co-written with Sven Hansen-Løve.
The film stars Félix de Givry and Pauline Etienne.
The film premiered in the Special Presentations section of the 2014 Toronto International Film Festival.
It was also screened in the 52nd edition of the New York Film Festival and the BFI London Film Festival.
The film's U.S. theatrical release was 19 June 2015.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

Her film "Father of My Children", won the Special Jury Prize in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2009 Cannes Film Festival.  In 2016, she won the Silver Bear for Best Director for her film "Things to Come" at the 66th Berlin International Film Festival.

All Is Forgiven (film) | All Is Forgiven (French: Tout est pardonné ) is a 2007 French drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.  It was screened in the Directors’ Fortnight section of the 2007 Cannes Film Festival.  It won the Louis Delluc Prize for Best First Film.  It was also nominated for the César Award for Best First Feature Film at the 2008 César Awards.

Eden (2014 French film) | Eden is a 2014 French drama film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve and co-written with Sven Hansen-Løve.  The film stars Félix de Givry and Pauline Etienne.  The film premiered in the Special Presentations section of the 2014 Toronto International Film Festival.  It was also screened in the 52nd edition of the New York Film Festival and the BFI London Film Festival.  The film's U.S. theatrical release was 19 June 2015.

Father of My Children | Father of My Children (French: Le Père de mes enfants ) is a 2009 French drama film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.  It won the Jury Special Prize in the Un Certain Regard section at the 2009 Cannes Film Festival.  It is based in part on the life of the late Humbert Balsan.

Goodbye First Love | Goodbye First Love (French: Un amour de jeunesse ) is a 2011 Franco-German film directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.  It was selected for the main competition section at the 2011 Locarno International Film Festival.

Alice de Lencquesaing | Alice de Lencquesaing (born 11 August 1991) is a French actress who appeared in Mia Hansen-Løve's 2009 film "Father of My Children" with her father Louis-Do de Lencquesaing.  She was also in the film "Summer Hours".

Things to Come (2016 film) | Things to Come (French title: L'Avenir) is a 2016 French-German drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.  It stars Isabelle Huppert as middle-aged philosophy professor Nathalie Chazeaux, whose life is going through a series of separations.
response.summary_1:
- [1] "won the Silver Bear for Best Director for her film 'Things to Come'" (paraphrase of passage [1]).
- [7] "It stars Isabelle Huppert" (quoted fragment from passage [7] about Things to Come).
- Bridge entity: the film "Things to Come" (2016) links Mia Hansen-Løve's award to the actor; present and unambiguous in passages [1] and [7].
- Conclusion: Mia Hansen-Løve's Silver Bear–winning film "Things to Come" starred Isabelle Huppert (supported by passage [7]).
response.hop2_query: Things to Come (2016) film starring who?
hop2_docs: Things to Come (2016 film) | Things to Come (French title: L'Avenir) is a 2016 French-German drama film written and directed by Mia Hansen-Løve.  It stars Isabelle Huppert as middle-aged philosophy professor Nathalie Chazeaux, whose life is going through a series of separations.

Things to Come | Things to Come (also known in promotional material as H. G. Wells' Things to Come) is a 1936 British black-and-white science fiction film from United Artists, produced by Alexander Korda, directed by William Cameron Menzies, and written by H. G. Wells.  The film stars Raymond Massey, Ralph Richardson, Cedric Hardwicke, Pearl Argyle, and Margaretta Scott.

I Am the Pretty Thing That Lives in the House | I Am the Pretty Thing That Lives in the House is a 2016 American-Canadian horror film written and directed by Osgood Perkins.  It stars Ruth Wilson as a live-in nurse who suspects her elderly employer's house may be haunted.  It premiered at the 2016 Toronto International Film Festival and was released worldwide on Netflix on October 28.

H. G. Wells' The Shape of Things to Come | H. G. Wells' The Shape of Things to Come is a 1979 Canadian science fiction film.  Although credited to H. G. Wells, the film takes only its title and some character names from "The Shape of Things to Come", Wells' speculative novel from 1933.  The film's plot has no relationship to the events of the book.  The book predicts events such as a Second World War and the collapse of social order until a world state is formed, whereas the film involves a high-tech future involving robots and spaceships.  The film was an attempt to capitalize on the popularity of such recent successes as "Star Wars", "Starcrash", and TV series such as "" and "Battlestar Galactica", although the film had only a fraction of the production budget of any of these.

I Love That Crazy Little Thing | I Love That Crazy Little Thing is a 2016 Chinese romantic comedy film directed by Snow Zou and starring William Chan, Tang Yixin and Jessica Jung.  It was released in China by EMP Distribution (Beijing) and Wanda Shengshi Film Distribution on August 12, 2016.

Best and Most Beautiful Things | Best and Most Beautiful Things is a 2016 documentary film directed by Garrett Zevgetis and produced by Zevgetis, Ariana Garfinkel, Jeff Consiglio, and Jordan Salvatoriello.  Its world premiere was held at the South by Southwest film festival on March 14, 2016, where it was nominated for a Jury Award for Best Documentary Feature.  The film explores the coming of age of an exuberant and precocious young blind woman, Michelle Smith, who identifies as being on the autism spectrum.  As she tries various ways of finding her true self in a new world of adults, she struggles with family tensions and trying to keep a job.  On a more personal level, she begins developing a sense of sexual identity through exploring aspects of fetishism.  In a December 1, 2016 review in The New York Times, Neil Genzlinger called the film "a remarkably forthright documentary" in which Ms. Smith makes "a difficult transition for someone with disabilities - the end of the schooling years, with their structure and relative safety."  The same review named the film a New York Times "Critics' Pick."

That Guy... Who Was in That Thing | That Guy... Who Was in That Thing is a 2012 documentary film by Ian Roumain and Michael Schwartz that features sixteen male character actors discussing their careers as working actors below the film star level but who are often recognized as being "that guy" who was in "that thing".  Two talent agents also comment on the challenges faced by such actors.
hop2_titles: I Am the Pretty Thing That Lives in the House
H. G. Wells' The Shape of Things to Come
I Love That Crazy Little Thing
That Guy... Who Was in That Thing
response.summary_2: Mia Hansen-Løve won the Silver Bear for Best Director for her film Things to Come. Things to Come (2016 film) stars Isabelle Huppert as middle-aged philosophy professor Nathalie Chazeaux.
